text: So in a very succinct way, embeddings are a way
of taking unstructured information,
as we've been seeing in the last few weeks, right?
Kind of like text sound images, video, et cetera, et cetera,
and to encode them.
And we've spent a bunch of time talking about this,
a bunch of time saying, hey, here are the images,
how do we represent an image as a mathematical object
that we can then feed to a machine learning algorithm?
And we say, okay, then an image is going to be a matrix
or in the case of a colored image,
three matrices, which values between zero and 255,
which of them encode a channel,
and each of the numbers represents the color
of a certain channel in a certain pixel, et cetera, et cetera.
And we say, okay, but if we are using words, text,
if we are parsing text,
then maybe we can represent the text
with the Begley-Cordes model,
which represents with a numeric vector,
the words that show up in a sentence or a book
or whatever piece of text we are analyzing.
But an embedding, this is an encoding, right?
Taking something that is not structured
as a mathematical object,
and encoding it as a mathematical object,
usually a vector or a matrix or something of that.
An embedding is a more sophisticated idea.
It's the idea that if we take a certain object
with unstructured information,
that we encode that object still
in a mathematical representation.
But in such a way that objects with similar semantics
have similar mathematical representation, right?
And these, if you think about it,
it's a really weird concept, right?
Because imagine that we are doing a Begley-Cordes, right?
And we have a sentence where I say,
Oh, sure.
The president of India went on a boat trip, right?
Or I say the sentence, sorry, not the president,
the prime minister.
The leader of India went on a boat trip.
And then I say the sentence,
the prime minister of,
the prime minister Modi,
sailed on a cruise, something like this.
Like these two sentences for all intents and purposes
are mean the same.
They have the same semantics, they mean the same thing.
Maybe there's a difference between what's a ship
and what's a cruise.
And if the sentence is said now,
maybe the prime minister of India,
or for 40 years ago, 50 years ago,
the prime minister of India was not Modi.
I mean, the point is these two sentences,
obviously have a very similar semantic context, right?
They mean you imagine the same picture in your head.
If they show up in a piece of news,
you kind of like you interpret them in the same way,
they represent the same concepts.
They have the same semantics.
And yet, when we think of them included, for example,
in a bag of words, they are quite different sentences, right?
Because in one case, we have the words,
prime minister and we have the words India.
And in the other, we have the word leader
and we have the word Modi.
And if we are doing a bag of words,
then our columns, if you recall,
are the different words that are used in the sentences.
And the words themselves are different,
even though they are synonyms,
even though they mean the same things,
and even though the sentence means a very similar thing,
the bag of words that represents it is quite different.
Now, don't get me wrong, bag of words,
it's a good encoding in the sense that it many times,
